description: Chapter manifest for BA: Law of the Firm / The Neon Edge

chapters.short_summary: Introduces the Four Problems — Attribution, Governance, Risk, and Partitioning — that shape every business entity. Zeeva arrives in New Boston 2077 and encounters the foundational questions that recur across the lifecycle of the firm.
learning_objectives: Identify the Four Problems that every business entity must resolve
Explain why law structures firms and allocates economic risk
Distinguish between principal, agent, and entity as legal concepts
Map the lifecycle stages of a business entity from formation to dissolution
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva arrives in New Boston and discovers the Four Problems that shape every business entity.

chapters.short_summary: Explores the law of agency — who acts on behalf of whom and who bears responsibility. Covers the control test, authority types, and respondeat superior through an interactive scenario involving Zeeva's first hire.
learning_objectives: Apply the control test to distinguish employees from independent contractors
Differentiate actual authority from apparent authority under RSA §2.01 and §2.03
Explain respondeat superior liability under RSA §7.07
Identify when a principal is bound by an agent's actions under RUPA §301
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva hires Sammy as a street fixer and learns who controls the bot — and who pays when it goes wrong.

chapters.short_summary: Examines how partnerships form by operation of law and the default rules governing partner relationships. Covers joint liability, fiduciary duties among partners, and the mechanics of dissolution under RUPA.
learning_objectives: Identify when a partnership forms by operation of law under RUPA §202
Explain joint and several liability of partners under RUPA §306
Describe fiduciary duties partners owe each other under RUPA §404
Analyze the causes and consequences of partnership dissolution under RUPA §801
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva's first handshake deal becomes a partnership by operation of law, bringing unlimited liability.

chapters.short_summary: Introduces the corporate form and its core innovation: entity shielding that partitions firm assets from personal creditors. Covers formation, board authority, and the foundational role of limited liability in enabling capital aggregation.
learning_objectives: Explain how incorporation creates an entity shield under DGCL §102
Describe the role of the board of directors under DGCL §141
Distinguish between authorized, issued, and outstanding shares under DGCL §151
Analyze how limited liability enables capital aggregation and risk allocation
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva incorporates to create an entity shield separating firm assets from personal creditors.

chapters.short_summary: Explores the LLC as a flexible hybrid entity that combines limited liability with contractual freedom. Examines how operating agreements shape governance and the tension between member-managed and manager-managed structures.
learning_objectives: Define the LLC and its core characteristics under ULLCA §102
Explain the scope of the operating agreement's contractual freedom under ULLCA §110
Compare member-managed and manager-managed governance under ULLCA §301
Analyze fiduciary duties in the LLC context under ULLCA §409
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva discovers the LLC's radical contractual freedom — and its governance trade-offs.

chapters.short_summary: Examines the nonprofit form and the nondistribution constraint that distinguishes it from for-profit entities. Explores how governance must serve mission when profit distribution is prohibited.
learning_objectives: Explain the nondistribution constraint and its effect on organizational governance
Describe the role of the board in nonprofit governance and mission enforcement
Identify the requirements for tax-exempt status under federal and state law
Compare nonprofit governance challenges to for-profit corporate governance
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva encounters organizations that cannot distribute profits, forcing governance to serve mission.

chapters.short_summary: Investigates Decentralized Autonomous Organizations and the legal challenges of code-as-law. Addresses attribution problems when governance is distributed through smart contracts and token voting.
learning_objectives: Define a DAO and explain how smart contracts automate governance
Analyze the attribution problem when no single agent controls the entity
Evaluate emerging DAO legislation including the Wyoming DAO Act
Assess the role of wrapper entities in providing legal certainty for DAOs
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva confronts code-as-law: when a DAO acts, who is the principal and who is the agent?

chapters.short_summary: Synthesizes prior chapters into a comparative entity-selection framework. Zeeva must weigh attribution, governance, risk, and partitioning trade-offs to choose the optimal form for her venture.
learning_objectives: Compare entity forms across all Four Problems dimensions
Evaluate the trade-offs between liability shielding, tax treatment, and governance flexibility
Apply a structured entity-selection framework to a realistic business scenario
Justify an entity choice by reference to specific statutory provisions
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva must choose the right entity form, balancing all Four Problems at once.

chapters.short_summary: Examines the loyalty and care duties owed by corporate directors and officers. Covers the business judgment rule, entire fairness review, and the role of good faith in mediating fiduciary obligations.
learning_objectives: Articulate the duties of loyalty and care under DGCL §141 and §144
Apply the business judgment rule and identify when it is rebutted
Explain entire fairness review and its burden-shifting mechanics
Analyze how the duty of good faith operates as a fiduciary constraint
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva faces the loyalty/care matrix when board decisions create conflicts of interest.

chapters.short_summary: Covers the regulatory framework for private capital raising, including Regulation D and Regulation A exemptions. Explores venture capital term sheets, preferred stock mechanics, and investor protections.
learning_objectives: Identify the key exemptions from securities registration under Regulation D and Regulation A
Define accredited investor status and its role in private placements
Analyze standard venture capital terms including anti-dilution and liquidation preferences
Evaluate the trade-offs of staying private versus going public
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva raises capital while staying private, navigating venture terms and investor protections.

chapters.short_summary: Traces the IPO process from registration statement to public trading. Examines how the transition to public company status transforms disclosure obligations, governance requirements, and the partitioning of ownership and control.
learning_objectives: Describe the registration and disclosure requirements for an IPO under the Securities Act of 1933
Explain the role of underwriters and market makers in the public offering process
Analyze how going public changes governance obligations under the Exchange Act of 1934
Evaluate the partitioning effects of dispersed public ownership
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva takes the firm public, crossing the threshold from private flexibility to public accountability.

chapters.short_summary: Examines the shareholder vote as the fundamental governance mechanism of the public corporation. Covers voting mechanics, proxy rules, cumulative voting, and the contested terrain of shareholder proposals.
learning_objectives: Explain voting rights and procedures under DGCL §211, §212, and §216
Describe the proxy system and its role in dispersed-ownership governance
Analyze cumulative voting and its impact on minority shareholder representation
Evaluate the legal framework for shareholder proposals and board elections

key_section_links.relevance: Consent of stockholders in lieu of meeting

chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva navigates the shareholder vote — the fundamental governance mechanism of the public corporation.

chapters.short_summary: Covers the law governing corporate combinations and hostile takeovers. Examines enhanced judicial scrutiny under Unocal and Revlon, the mechanics of mergers and tender offers, and the cleansing effect of Corwin.
learning_objectives: Describe the statutory framework for mergers under DGCL §251 and asset sales under §271
Apply the Unocal enhanced scrutiny test to defensive measures
Explain Revlon duties and when they are triggered
Analyze the Corwin cleansing doctrine and appraisal rights as shareholder remedies
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva faces a hostile takeover bid — triggering enhanced judicial scrutiny of board loyalty.

chapters.short_summary: Examines when courts disregard the corporate entity to hold shareholders personally liable. Covers alter ego doctrine, undercapitalization, commingling, and the boundaries of entity shielding.
learning_objectives: Identify the factors courts consider when deciding to pierce the corporate veil
Apply the alter ego and instrumentality doctrines to fact patterns
Explain how undercapitalization and commingling undermine entity shielding
Analyze the tension between limited liability and creditor protection
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva watches the corporate veil dissolve when entity boundaries are abused.

chapters.short_summary: Explores the legal rules governing a firm's capital structure, including the debt-equity distinction, dividend restrictions, and fraudulent transfer law. Addresses when capital structure decisions create risk for creditors.
learning_objectives: Distinguish debt from equity and explain the legal implications of each under DGCL §151
Analyze dividend restrictions and capital maintenance rules under DGCL §160 and §170
Apply fraudulent transfer principles under the UVTA to assess solvency
Evaluate how capital structure decisions allocate risk between equity holders and creditors

key_section_links.relevance: Transfers fraudulent as to present and future creditors

chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva confronts the creditor's dilemma: when the firm's capital structure threatens solvency.

chapters.short_summary: Synthesizes the entire course by revisiting the Four Problems across every entity form and lifecycle stage. Zeeva completes her journey with a unified theory of the firm and its professional responsibilities.
learning_objectives: Synthesize the Four Problems framework across all entity forms and lifecycle stages
Evaluate how attribution, governance, risk, and partitioning interact in complex transactions
Articulate a unified theory of the firm grounded in doctrinal analysis
Identify professional responsibility obligations arising from entity representation
chapters.narrative_hook: Zeeva completes the lifecycle, synthesizing all Four Problems into a unified theory of the firm.
